coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots sensitized lanthanide infinite coordination polymer nanoparticles: Towards ratiometric fluorescent sensing of cerebrospinal Aβ monomer as a biomarker for Alzheimer’s disease
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Chang
Lu, Dingkun
You, Xinrui
Shi, Guoyue
Deng, Jingjing
Zhou, Tianshu
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a novel ratiometric fluorescent probe based on CDs@Eu/GMP ICP nanoparticles was developed for the detection of Aβ monomer in rat as a biomarker for Alzheimer’s disease (AD) by fully exploring the competitive coordination interaction and by taking advantage of excellent optical property of carbon dots sensitized lanthanide infinite coordination polymer (ICP) nanoparticles. The carbon dots (CDs) with abundant functional groups were encapsulated into Eu/GMP ICPs through self-adaptive chemistry, which could not only sensitize the red fluorescence of Eu/GMP ICPs effectively, but also act as an internal reference for self-correction. In the absence of Cu2+, the as-formed CDs@Eu/GMP ICPs exhibited the characteristic emission of CDs at 400 nm and strong emission of Eu3+ at 592 nm, 615 nm, 650 nm and 694 nm. With the addition of Cu2+, the red fluorescence of Eu3+ decreased due to the coordination interaction between CDs and Cu2+, thus destroyed the antenna effect. After the subsequent addition of Aβ monomer, the specific binding occurred between Cu2+ and Aβ monomer, and then the red fluorescence of Eu3+ restored again. During this process, the fluorescence of CDs remained unchanged, thus could be used as an internal reference to cancel out the environmental fluctuation and was more adaptive for the detection of Aβ monomer in biological fluids. The method demonstrated here was highly sensitive, free from the interference of other species in rat brain, the in vivo analysis of Aβ monomer in CSF and different brain regions from normal rats and Alzheimer’s rats could be realized, which was of great significance for better understanding the mechanism of AD and paving the way to understand the chemical essence involved in AD.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescent probe
CDs@Eu/GMP infinite coordination polymer nanoparticles
Aβ monomer
Competitive coordination

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescent sensing of Aβ monomer in the brain of normal rats and Alzheimer’s rats
Characterization of Eu/GMP ICP nanoparticles, CDs and CDs@Eu/GMP ICP nanoparticles
Mechanism of CDs@Eu/GMP ICP nanoprobe toward Aβ monomer detection
Sensing Aβ monomer in CSF and brain tissues of normal rats and Alzheimer’s rats

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images and photograph (inset) of Eu/GMP ICP nanoparticles; (B) TEM images of CDs. Inset: photograph (left) and HR-TEM images (right); (C) TEM images of CDs@Eu/GMP ICP nanoparticles. Inset: photograph (left) and HR-TEM images (right); (D) Fluorescence spectra of Eu/GMP ICP nanoparticles, CDs and CDs@Eu/GMP ICP nanoparticles, respectively.

(A) FTIR spectra of CDs (a, black curve), Eu/GMP ICP nanoparticles (b, red curve), CDs@Eu/GMP ICP nanoparticles (c, blue curve) and CDs@Eu/GMP + Cu2+ (d, green curve); (B) XPS spectrum of CDs, Eu/GMP ICP nanoparticles and CDs@Eu/GMP ICP nanoparticles; (C) Fluorescence decay profiles of CDs and CDs@Eu/GMP ICP nanoparticles. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Fluorescence spectra of CDs@Eu/GMP, CDs@Eu/GMP + Cu2+ and CDs@Eu/GMP + Cu2+ + Aβ monomer. Inset photograph: (1) CDs@Eu/GMP, (2) CDs@Eu/GMP + Cu2+, (3) CDs@Eu/GMP + Cu2+ + Aβ monomer.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of mixture of CDs@Eu/GMP ICP and Cu2+ after the addition of various concentrations of Aβ monomer; (B) The calibration plot of F 615/F 400 versus the concentration of Aβ monomer; (C) The ratio of F 615/F 400 versus the various interferences (20 nM Aβ oligomer, 20 nM Aβ aggregate, 1 μM α-syn, 1 μM IAPP, 1 μg/mL TYR, 0.26 μg/mL LZM, aCSF, 2 μM GSH, 2 μM AA, 2 μM DA, 2 μM Hcys, 2 μM Cys, 2 μM L-Asp, 2 μM L-Glu, 6 μM L-Arg, 12 μM L-Ser, 18 μM L-Ala and the mixture of above interfering substances). Conditions: ICP, 0.67 mg mL−1; Cu2+, 60 μM; reaction time, 20 min; pH, 7.4; λex, 280 nm.
Synthetic route for CDs@Eu/GMP ICP nanoparticles and the mechanism for ratiometric fluorescent sensing of Aβ monomer based on the specific reaction between Cu2+ and Aβ monomer.
Determination of Aβ monomer in normal and AD rats brains based on ratiometric CDs@Eu/GMP ICP nanoprobe (mean ± SD, n = 5).

head.ce:title: Carbon dots sensitized lanthanide infinite coordination polymer nanoparticles: Towards ratiometric fluorescent sensing of cerebrospinal Aβ monomer as a biomarker for Alzheimer’s disease

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Ecological and Environmental Sciences
Shanghai Key Lab for Urban Ecological Process and Eco-Restoration

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Institute of Eco-Chongming, 3663 Zhongshan Road, Shanghai, 200062, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a novel ratiometric fluorescent probe based on CDs@Eu/GMP ICP nanoparticles was developed for the detection of Aβ monomer in rat as a biomarker for Alzheimer’s disease (AD) by fully exploring the competitive coordination interaction and by taking advantage of excellent optical property of carbon dots sensitized lanthanide infinite coordination polymer (ICP) nanoparticles. The carbon dots (CDs) with abundant functional groups were encapsulated into Eu/GMP ICPs through self-adaptive chemistry, which could not only sensitize the red fluorescence of Eu/GMP ICPs effectively, but also act as an internal reference for self-correction. In the absence of Cu2+, the as-formed CDs@Eu/GMP ICPs exhibited the characteristic emission of CDs at 400 nm and strong emission of Eu3+ at 592 nm, 615 nm, 650 nm and 694 nm. With the addition of Cu2+, the red fluorescence of Eu3+ decreased due to the coordination interaction between CDs and Cu2+, thus destroyed the antenna effect. After the subsequent addition of Aβ monomer, the specific binding occurred between Cu2+ and Aβ monomer, and then the red fluorescence of Eu3+ restored again. During this process, the fluorescence of CDs remained unchanged, thus could be used as an internal reference to cancel out the environmental fluctuation and was more adaptive for the detection of Aβ monomer in biological fluids. The method demonstrated here was highly sensitive, free from the interference of other species in rat brain, the in vivo analysis of Aβ monomer in CSF and different brain regions from normal rats and Alzheimer’s rats could be realized, which was of great significance for better understanding the mechanism of AD and paving the way to understand the chemical essence involved in AD.

ce:section.ce:para: Alzheimer’s disease (AD) is a central nervous system degenerative disease marked by the loss of ability in memory, judgment and abstract thinking [1]. According to the latest World Alzheimer Report 2018, this disease has affected about 20 million people worldwide and this figure is estimated to increase to 152 million people by 2050. Consequently, early diagnosis of the biomarker of AD in the brain is of great research value for the prevention and treatment of AD.

It is generally believed that the β-amyloid (Aβ) peptide formed by the proteolytic cleavage of amyloid precursor protein (APP) plays an important role in the pathogenesis of AD, which is a 4 kDa peptide consisting of 39–43 amino acids that is present in rat brain tissues and cerebrospinal fluid [2,3]. The native form of Aβ is unfolded, but it tends to aggregate into oligomer or fibril under various conditions, which is the main cause of cognitive decline in Alzheimer’s patients [4,5]. Therefore, the level of extracellular Aβ monomer in the brain is considered as an important biomarker in assessing the cognitive impairment of AD [6]. Accordingly, the development of simple and efficient assays to observe variation of Aβ monomer level in the brain could provide effective tools for better understanding the mechanism of AD [7,8].

In recent years, multiple approaches have already been employed to detect Aβ monomer, among which, fluorescent assays are particularly attractive for Aβ monomer detection due to their high sensitivity, good selectivity and fast response. For example, Xu’s group proposed a highly sensitive and selective lanthanide metal-organic coordination polymer (LMOCP) sensor with turn-on fluorescence for sensing of Aβ1−40 monomer in human plasma [9]. In addition, a novel and effective graphitic carbon nitride dots-based fluorescent strategy was developed by Yu’s group for Aβ monomer determination with low detection limit down to ng/mL level [10]. However, most of the fluorescent methods depend on the intensity change at a single wavelength, the signal output could be easily overshadowed by the background noise of the sample, especially in biological fluids. To enhance the reliability of fluorescent sensing, ratiometric fluorescent assay with relative fluorescence intensities at two different wavelengths are highly desired. Recently, lanthanide-based infinite coordination polymer (Ln-ICPs) have been emerging as very attractive nanomaterials due to their unique spectral characteristics, such as large Stokes shifts, long fluorescence lifetime, high photochemical stability, which make them very suitable for time-resolved luminescence detection and readily distinguish from the background luminescence in many fields [11,12]. More interestingly, Ln-ICPs are amorphous nanoparticles with superior adaptive capability of guest encapsulation [13,14]. The as-formed Ln-ICPs combine the inherent properties of both the porous network and functional guest (i.e., carbon dots), which could be beneficial to realize ratiometric fluorescent sensing and are more adaptive for complex brain system with the ability of built-in correction.

In this work, a novel ratiometric fluorescent assay was established for the detection of Aβ monomer in rat as a biomarker for Alzheimer’s disease with high sensitivity and reliability based on CDs@Eu/GMP ICP nanoparticles (Scheme 1 ). The CDs with abundant amino and carboxyl groups were synthesized via hydrothermal carbonization of chitosan and could emit strong blue fluorescence under UV lamp irradiation (λex = 365 nm). The synthetic CDs could act as an effective antenna ligand to facilitate the self-assembly behavior of Eu3+ and GMP to form CDs@Eu/GMP ICP nanoparticles accompanying with sensitized red fluorescence of Eu/GMP ICPs. The as-formed CDs@Eu/GMP ICP nanoparticles exhibited both the characteristic emission of CDs at 400 nm and the characteristic emission of Eu3+ at 592 nm, 615 nm, 650 nm and 694 nm. In the presence of Cu2+, the coordination interaction between CDs and Cu2+ disrupted the antenna effect, leading to the fluorescence quenching of Eu3+. However, with the subsequent addition of Aβ monomer, the stronger coordination between Aβ monomer and Cu2+ restored the red fluorescence of Eu3+. During this process, the fluorescence of CDs remained unchanged, thus could be served as an internal reference to achieve built-in correction, which essentially constituted a new mechanism for ratiometric fluorescent detection of Aβ monomer. Moreover, by ultrafiltration, the method was free from the interferences of other species in rat brain. The excellent analytical properties ensured further application of this assay for the determination of Aβ monomer in CSF and various brain tissues from normal rats and Alzheimer’s rats, which was envisaged to pave a new avenue to monitor brain chemistry related to Alzheimer’s disease.

Europium Nitrate Hexahydrate (Eu(NO3)3·6H2O), Glutathione (GSH), l-cysteine (Cys), l-arginine (L-Arg), Ascorbic acid (AA), Dopamine (DA), Homocysteine (Hcys), d-galactose (D-gal) and Cupric chloride (CuCl2) were purchased from Aladdin reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Chitosan and Islet amyloid polypeptide (IAPP) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). 5′-guanosine monophosphate disodium (GMP), N-2-Hydroxyethylpiperazine-N′-2-ethane-sulfonic acid (HEPES), α-synuclein peptide (α-syn), Tyrosinase (TYR) and Lysozyme (LZM) were purchased from Sangon Biotech Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). l-serine (L-Ser) and Chloralhydrate were obtained from J & K Scientific. Ltd (Beijing, China). l-aspartic acid (L-Asp), l-glutamic acid (L-Glu) and l-alanine (L-Ala) were purchased from TCI Development Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Ibotenic acid (IBO) was obtained from Abcam Trading Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). The test kits of malondialdehyde (MDA), superoxide dismutase (SOD) and catalase (CAT) were obtained from Beyotime Institute of Biotechnology (Shanghai, China). Purified synthetic β-amyloid peptides were purchased from Chinapeptides Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). A stock solution of Aβ monomer (1 mg mL−1) was dissolved in water and adjusted pH to 7.4. All chemicals were of analytical purity and used without further purification. All aqueous solutions were prepared with Milli-Q water (18.2 MΩ cm).

Fluorescence spectra were recorded on F-4600 spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan). The fluorescence lifetime was characterized with an Edinburgh fluorescence spectrometer (FLS980). Photographs were taken by Canon EOS 550D digital camera. The FT-IR spectrophotometer (Nicolet 560, Nicolet, America) was applied to record Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were carried out on a TEM (JEM-2011F), operating at 200 kV. The elemental composition and chemical oxidation state were investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250 XI, Thermo Scientific, America) with Al Ka radiation as the excitation source (hν = 1486.6 eV).

Initially, the CDs@Eu/GMP ICP suspension was prepared by dispersing CDs@Eu/GMP ICP nanoparticles (33.5 mg) in 5.0 mL HEPES buffer (50 mM, pH 7.4) under ultrasonication for 15 min. For fluorescent detection of Aβ monomer, an aqueous solution of Cu2+ (900 μL, 100 μM) was firstly added into the dispersion of CDs@Eu/GMP ICP nanoparticles (150 μL, 6.7 mg mL−1). After reacting 4 min, 300 μL of different concentrations of Aβ monomer were added to the reaction mixtures. The total volume was fixed at 1500 μL by adding the HEPES buffer (50 mM, pH 7.4). The fluorescent sensing of Aβ monomer using the CDs@Eu/GMP ICP nanoprobe was performed by mixing different concentrations of Aβ standard solution or brain samples with the total volume at a volume ratio of 1:5. The resulting dispersion were allowed to stand by for 20 min, and then used for fluorescence spectroscopic measurements. The final concentrations of Aβ monomer in the resulting mixtures were 0.5 nM–100 nM, respectively.

Thereafter, the selectivity of as-formed CDs@Eu/GMP ICP nanoprobe for Aβ monomer was investigated by adding separate Aβ oligomer (20 nM), Aβ aggregate (20 nM), α-syn (1 μM), IAPP (1 μM), TYR (1 μg/mL), LZM (0.26 μg/mL), artificial cerebrospinal fluid (aCSF, consist of 126 mM NaCl, 2.4 mM KCl, 0.5 mM KH2PO4, 0.85 mM MgCl2, 27.5 mM NaHCO3, 0.5 mM Na2SO4 and 1.1 mM CaCl2), GSH (2 μM), AA (2 μM), DA (2 μM), Hcys (2 μM), Cys (2 μM), L-Asp (2 μM), L-Glu (2 μM), L-Arg (6 μM), L-Ser (12 μM), L-Ala (18 μM) and the mixture of above interfering substances into the mixture containing CDs@Eu/GMP ICP nanoparticles (0.67 mg mL−1), Cu2+ (60 μM) and HEPES buffer (50 mM, pH 7.4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent sensing of Aβ monomer in the brain of normal rats and Alzheimer’s rats
ce:section.ce:para: For fluorescent sensing of Aβ monomer in CSF and various brain tissues, a reaction mixture containing CDs@Eu/GMP ICP nanoparticles (0.67 mg mL−1), Cu2+ (60 μM) and HEPES buffer (50 mM, pH 7.4) was first prepared. The CSF and supernatants of the homogenates were centrifuged in 3 kDa ultrafiltration tubes to remove inorganic salts and other small molecules. Next, the retained solution in ultrafiltration tubes was added into the reaction mixture and the resulting mixture were reacted for 20 min and the fluorescence spectrum of reaction mixture were recorded at excitation wavelength of 280 nm, and the fluorescent emission at 400 nm and 615 nm were monitored.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of Eu/GMP ICP nanoparticles, CDs and CDs@Eu/GMP ICP nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, the morphology and luminescence of Eu/GMP ICP nanoparticles, CDs and CDs@Eu/GMP ICP nanoparticles were characterized. As shown in Fig. 1 A, Eu/GMP ICP nanoparticles were colloidal spheres with diameters ranging from 40 to 50 nm. Due to the Laporte-forbidden transitions and the resultant low absorption coefficients, Eu/GMP ICP nanoparticles showed quite weak luminescence (Fig. 1A, inset), which rendered difficulty for fluorescent analysis. To solve this problem, the suitable guest, which could replace the coordinating water molecules and strengthen intermolecular energy transfer, was taken into account. CDs with abundant functional groups was the good candidate to realize this goal. As shown in Fig. 1B, the synthetic CDs were well-dispersed with a particle size of about 1.6 ± 0.2 nm. The high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 1B, inset) further indicated the d space value of 0.21 nm, which agreed with (100) planes of graphitic carbon. When being excited at 280 nm, the CDs emitted strong blue fluorescence at 400 nm (Fig. 1D, black curve). After being encapsulated into Eu/GMP ICP network, the morphology of as-formed CDs@Eu/GMP ICP nanoparticles were almost the same as that of Eu/GMP ICP particles, suggesting that the encapsulation of CDs (Fig. 1C, inset) did not affect the structure of Eu/GMP ICP network (Fig. 1C). However, with the encapsulation of CDs, a strong red luminescence of Eu3+ could be observed for CDs@Eu/GMP ICP nanoparticles (Fig. 1C, inset), with peaks at 592 nm, 615 nm, 650 nm and 694 nm (Fig. 1D, blue curve), while the blue fluorescence of CDs at 400 nm decreased, indicating the interaction between the entrapped CDs with Eu3+ may strongly promote the 4f -4f electron transitions of Eu3+, thus may gave rise to sensitize Eu3+ emission.

To further verify the encapsulation of CDs and explain the enhanced fluorescence of CDs@Eu/GMP ICP nanoparticles more clearly, Fourier transfer infrared (FT-IR) spectra of CDs, Eu/GMP ICP and CDs@Eu/GMP ICP nanoparticles were investigated. As shown in Fig. 2 A, for free CDs (a, black curve), the absorption peak at 1059 cm−1 and 1780 cm−1 were assigned to the C–O and CO stretching vibration of carboxylic group. And the absorption at 3325 cm−1 was ascribed to the stretching vibration of the free O–H bonds in carboxyl group. In addition, the absorption peak at 1651 cm−1 was assigned to the deformational vibration of –NH2 [15]. In addition, the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) plot of CDs was determined (Fig. 2B) to explore their composition and chemical bonds. Three peaks, at 284.8, 399.9, and 532.1 eV, were attributed to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively. The XPS spectra of C 1s, O 1s and N 1s (Fig. S3) further confirmed the presence of C–O (286.4 eV), COOH (288.1 eV), CO (532.1 eV) and C–N (399.9 eV) bonds in CDs [16,17]. All of these results indicated the presence of rich functional groups (-COOH and –NH2) on the surface of CDs. For Eu/GMP ICP (b, red curve), the absorption peak at 3717 cm−1 was attributed to the O–H stretching vibration of coordinated water molecules in the Eu/GMP network structure. Besides, the characteristic peak of PO4 3− of GMP appeared at 970 cm−1 [18]. Nevertheless, after being incorporated into Eu/GMP ICP particles, the characteristic absorption peaks of CDs at 1059 cm−1, 1780 cm−1 and 1651 cm−1 disappeared (c, blue curve), suggesting CDs may be encapsulated in the interior of Eu/ICP network rather than simply absorbed on the surface, thus the vibration of CDs would be limited due to the shielding effect. Moreover, the characteristic O–H band of coordinated water molecules at 3717 cm−1 could not be observed while the characteristic O–H band of carboxyl group on CDs red-shifted to 3582 cm−1, indicating the replacement of the coordinating water molecules in Eu/GMP ICP nanoparticles by the possible coordination interaction between CDs and Eu3+ and thus may facilitate energy transfer and finally sensitized the luminescence of Eu/GMP. To confirm this speculation, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and fluorescence lifetime of CDs and CDs@Eu/GMP ICP nanoparticles were next performed. As shown in Fig. 2B, the O 1s of CDs@Eu/GMP ICP nanoparticles exhibited a higher binding energy (532.2 eV) than Eu/GMP (532.14 eV), while the Eu 4d peaks appeared at 134.81 eV were lower than the Eu/GMP (136.8 eV). This phenomenon could be owing to the formation of Eu–O coordination bonds between the carboxyl group of CDs and Eu3+ [19]. Moreover, after incorporation, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs (3.2 ns) shortened to 2.4 ns, demonstrating the coordination interaction happened between CDs and Eu/GMP and thus essentially promoted the energy transfer from CDs to Eu3+ (Fig. 2C) [20].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of CDs@Eu/GMP ICP nanoprobe toward Aβ monomer detection
ce:section.ce:para: As depicted in Scheme 1, the competitive coordination interaction of Cu2+ between guest CDs and Aβ monomer was designed to achieve ratiometric detection of Aβ monomer. With the addition of Cu2+ (60 μM), the fluorescence of CDs remained unchanged while the fluorescence of Eu3+ quenched, accompanying with the decrease of the fluorescent emission at 615 nm (Fig. 3 , inset, from vial 1 to via 2). It also could be explained by FT-IR spectra. As shown in Fig. 2A, for CDs@Eu/GMP ICPs + Cu2+ (green curve), relative to CDs@Eu/GMP ICPs (blue curve), the characteristic absorption peaks of CDs appeared at 3419 cm−1, 1055 cm−1, 1770 cm−1 and 1614 cm−1, respectively. Moreover, compared with Eu/GMP ICPs (red curve), the O–H stretching vibration of free water molecules appeared at 3732 cm−1, implying the coordination interaction may occur between the functional group of CDs and Cu2+ and the free water molecules gradually entered the Eu/GMP network structure, leading to the disruption of antenna effect and the fluorescence of Eu3+ quenched [21,22]. However, upon the addition of Aβ monomer into the as-formed CDs@Eu/GMP ICP nanoparticles dispersion with the presence of Cu2+, the fluorescence of Eu3+ recovered obviously (Fig. 3, inset, vial 2 to vial 3). According to the literatures, the binding constant (K) of the complex formed by Cu2+ and Aβ1-40 monomer is as high as 6.25 × 105 M−1 and that for the complex formed by Cu2+ and Aβ1-42 monomer is K = 5.0 × 105 M−1 [23,24]. The stronger binding ability between Aβ monomer and Cu2+ destructed the coordination interaction between CDs and Cu2+, thus the antenna effect resulted in the recovery of the red fluorescence of Eu3+ and the increased fluorescent intensity at 615 nm (F 615). In addition, during the competitive coordination interaction of Cu2+ between CDs and Aβ monomer, the fluorescence of CDs at 400 nm remained unchanged, thus F 400 could be served as an internal reference to achieve built-in correction for the ratiometric fluorescent detection of Aβ monomer, as will be demonstrated below.

To ensure the optimal performance of CDs@Eu/GMP ICP nanoprobe, the concentration of Cu2+, pH value, the amount of ICP nanoparticles and the reaction time were all optimized before Aβ monomer detection (Figs. S4 and S5). Under the optimum conditions (ICP: 0.67 mg mL−1; Cu2+: 60 μM; reaction time: 20 min; pH: 7.4), as depicted in Fig. 4 A, with the addition of Aβ monomer to the mixture of CDs@Eu/GMP ICP nanoprobes and Cu2+ (60 μM), the fluorescence of Eu3+ turned on. F 615 increased gradually with the increasing of Aβ monomer concentration, while F 400 remained almost unchanged. The ratio of F 615/F 400 was linear against Aβ monomer concentration within the range from 0.5 nM to 100 nM with a correlation coefficient of 0.993 (Fig. 4B). The detection limit was calculated to be 0.17 nM based on the ratio of signal-to-noise of 3, which could meet the detection requirement of Aβ monomer level (nM) in cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) and various brain tissues of rats. Furthermore, the response of CDs@Eu/GMP ICP nanoparticles towards Aβ monomer was stable since the F 615 /F 400 was nearly unchanged as it was used as nanoprobe to detect Aβ monomer within consecutive 7 days, demonstrating their good stability (Fig. S5D).

Because of the complexity of brain samples, not only the high sensitivity, but also the good selectivity is required for the analysis of Aβ monomer. To evaluate the performance of the developed method, the interference of different species coexisted in brain were studied [10,25–29]. As shown in Fig. 4C, small molecules with potential interference such as different amino acids (Cys, L-Asp, L-Glu, L-Arg, L-Ser and L-Ala) and endogenic substances (GSH, AA, DA and Hcys) were firstly investigated, we observed that the mixture of CDs@Eu/GMP ICP nanoprobe and Cu2+ did not show an obvious response either in the fluorescence intensity or the ratio of F 615/F 400, suggesting that these small molecules did not interfere with the detection of Aβ monomer in rat brain. Apart from the small molecules mentioned above, some biological molecules with an approximate molecular weight of Aβ monomer including Aβ oligomer, Aβ aggregate, α-syn, IAPP, TYR and LZM were also selected for the interference test. As depicted in Fig. 4C, no obvious response was observed following the additions of these interferences into the mixture of CDs@Eu/GMP ICP nanoprobe and Cu2+, indicating that these substances also did not cause interference to the determination of Aβ monomer. In addition, the stability of CDs@Eu/GMP ICP nanoprobe with the presence of aCSF was also investigated due to the presence of metal cations and high salinity in aCSF. As displayed in Fig. 4C, the presence of aCSF did not influence the ratio of F 615/F 400, suggesting that the aCSF did not interfere with the Aβ monomer sensing in rat brain, which was consistent with the reported literature [21]. Note that, from Fig. S8, the separate addition of different metal cations (Na+, K+, Mg2+ and Ca2+) to the dispersion of CDs@Eu/GMP ICP nanoprobe did not cause obvious fluorescence decrease of CDs@Eu/GMP ICP and the ratio of F 615/F 400 was the same as blank, while the ratio of F 615/F 400 decreased significantly with the presence of Cu2+, demonstrating that the CDs@Eu/GMP ICP nanoprobe exhibited high salt tolerance, which ensured their stability even with the presence of other metal cations. Besides, compared to the separate addition of the possible interfering species, the simultaneous presence of all these possible interfering substances also did not cause obvious influence to the ratio of F615/F400, suggesting our probe exhibited a good selectivity towards Aβ monomer in rat brain (Fig. 4C). However, due to the intrinsic chemical complexity of the brain system, it is really hard for us to investigate all of the potential interferences. To ensure the reliability of our results, ultrafiltration tube with a cut-off molecular mass of 3 kDa was then applied to rule out the possible influence of any small molecules in rat brain with binding ability towards Cu2+ [30,31]. As displayed in Fig. 4C, after ultrafiltration, the mixture of Aβ monomer and all the possible interfering substances did not show any fluorescence difference with those achieved by sole presence of Aβ monomer (100 nM), suggesting the operation of ultrafiltration did not result in an impact on the Aβ monomer analysis and again validating the reliability of our results. This superior selectivity, along with the good linearity, substantially enables the further application of the developed ratiometric fluorescent assay for monitoring Aβ monomer level in brain of normal rats and AD rats.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing Aβ monomer in CSF and brain tissues of normal rats and Alzheimer’s rats
ce:section.ce:para: It has been reported that the activity of SOD and CAT, as well as the MDA content were all involved in the process of AD with d-galactose and IBO treated rats, so these indicators were checked to demonstrate the successful modeling of AD rats. Table S1 summarized the variations in SOD, CAT activities and MDA content between the normal group and AD group. Compared with normal rats, the activities of SOD and CAT in the hippocampus, prefrontal cortex and striatum decreased (P < 0.0001, highly significant), and the content of MDA increased in model rats we established (P < 0.0001, highly significant), which indicated that the neurons of hippocampus, prefrontal cortex and striatum in model rats have undergone degradation, thus may finally build the close relationship with AD (Table S1). Meanwhile, the amyloid plaques deposited in different brain regions were the direct proof of AD model rats. As shown in Fig. S6A and Fig. S7A, there was no obvious amyloid plaque in the prefrontal cortex of normal rats, while a large number of amyloid plaques were observed in the prefrontal cortex of model rats (Fig. S6B and Fig. S7B). Fig. S6C and Fig. S7C also showed that the hippocampal vertebral cells in normal rat brains were closely arranged and regular. However, with the increasing in the concentration of Aβ aggregates, the hippocampal vertebral cells in brains became thinner and the gap became larger in model rats (Fig. S6D and Fig. S7D). All of these results validated the successful modeling of AD rats.

Having demonstrated the successful modeling of AD rats, the ratiometric fluorescence assay based on the CDs@Eu/GMP ICPs was further applied to monitor the level of Aβ monomer in CSF and brain tissues of normal and AD rats for assessing the practicability. The results are shown in Table 1 . The concentration of Aβ monomer in three different brain regions of AD rats were much lower than normal rats, especially for hippocampus and prefrontal cortex (decreased by 57.6% and 59.1%, respectively). The results were in accordance with the pathological feature of AD - the extracellular deposition of Aβ aggregates in the form of senile plaques (Figs. S6A and B and Figs. S7A and B), which finally caused cognitive decline and memory loss [32–34]. Meanwhile, the result was consistent with the literature that Aβ monomer level may be inversely related to the concentration of Aβ aggregates in brain tissues and thus decreased with AD progression [35,36]. Moreover, as listed in Table 1, the concentration of Aβ monomer in the CSF of normal rats was 17 nM, while that in CSF of AD rats was 8.2 nM, which was 51.8% lower than in normal rats. The level of Aβ monomer in CSF showed similar decline as in brain tissues of AD rats, which was correlated with the pathological characteristics of AD [6,37]. Because AD pathology is restricted to the brain, CSF is an ideal source that reflects the biochemical changes of brain. The CSF sample is much more accessible to obtain for in vivo analysis of Aβ monomer. The determination of cerebrospinal Aβ monomer could reflect central nervous system diseases more directly and sensitively than neuroimaging technique, which is more reliable for early diagnosis of AD and exploring the mechanism of AD [38,39]. In addition, the cerebrospinal Aβ monomer is expected to meet great clinical requirements to identify incipient AD patients. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first example of Ln-ICP based assay with ratiometric fluorescence to realize cerebrospinal Aβ monomer sensing in normal rats and AD rats, which is of great significance for early diagnosis and treatment of AD.

In this work, by taking advantage of self-adaptive property of the Eu/GMP network and rationally designing the competitive coordination interaction of Cu2+ between the guest CDs and Aβ monomer, a novel method for ratiometric detection of cerebrospinal Aβ monomer as a biomarker of AD have been developed. The functional guest CDs served both as the antenna ligand to endow Eu-ICP host with superior optical property and as an internal reference to be provided the developed assay with self-correction ability. With the method employed here, the determination of cerebrospinal Aβ monomer from normal rats and AD rats could be realized with high sensitivity, selectivity and reliability. The successful determination of Aβ monomer in the cerebral system is envisaged to be applicable for the early diagnosis of AD and better understanding the chemical essence involved in AD disease.

Chang Liu: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Dingkun Lu: Data curation, Investigation. Xinrui You: Data curation, Investigation. Guoyue Shi: Methodology, Resources, Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Jingjing Deng: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research is financially supported by the Key Project of Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality (Grant 18DZ1112700), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2016M590336 and 2017T100286).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2020.01.021.
